# Locality Guide: Port of Boston — Immigration Records (1840s)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Place:** Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts, United States (city incorporated 1630)
- **Administrative center:** Boston is the county seat of Suffolk County and the state capital of Massachusetts
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Suffolk County → Commonwealth of Massachusetts
- **Context:** In the 1840s Boston was one of the five principal U.S. ports of entry. The period coincides with the onset of the Great Famine in Ireland (1845–1852), which drove a surge in transatlantic arrivals. By 1850, over a quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born, according to Wikipedia's Boston article. Other arrivals included English, German, and Canadian immigrants. Newcomers concentrated in the North End and Fort Hill neighborhoods.
- **Key event:** The Famine migration of 1845–1852 is the defining immigration event of this period for the port.

---

## Boundary Notes

Boston's city limits expanded through annexation during and after the 1840s, but Suffolk County remained the civil jurisdiction overseeing vital events, court proceedings, and naturalizations throughout the period. No record-splitting boundary changes affect the 1840s window.

---

## Available Record Types

### 1. Customs Passenger Lists (Port of Boston) ⭐ Primary Source

- **What they are:** Lists submitted to the U.S. Customs Service by ship captains upon arrival, mandated by the Passenger Act of 1819. The Port of Boston lists covering 1820–1891 are held in **National Archives Record Group 36**.
- **1840s content:** Name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin. Unlike post-1895 manifests, 1840s lists do **not** routinely record a specific town of origin, last residence, or destination contact — information that would only appear in later arrivals.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891* (collection ID 1784312) — reported at 1,234,567 records with 617,283 images on FamilySearch. *(Record count as reported by the tool; treat as approximate.)*
- **FamilySearch volume:** Image group **003345210** — 389 images specifically covering **Boston passenger lists, 1840–1850**. This volume is **3% name-indexed** (essentially not searchable by name index alone) but is **full-text searchable**, meaning you can search it using FamilySearch's full-text search even without a name index. Browse image-by-image is also possible.
- **Originals:** National Archives at Boston (NARA–Waltham), 380 Trapelo Road, Waltham, MA — holds the original manifests.
- **Also on Ancestry:** The same series is available and indexed on Ancestry.com under U.S. passenger list collections.
- **Source:** FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Immigration*, "Port of Boston" section.

---

### 2. The Boston Pilot "Missing Persons" Database ⭐ Extraordinary Irish Resource

- **What it is:** *The Pilot* was Boston's Irish Catholic newspaper. From 1831 to 1921 it published "Missing Persons" notices placed by immigrants searching for relatives separated during the Famine migration. The database has been compiled by the New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS) and is searchable free online at **infowanted.bc.edu** (Boston College).
- **Coverage:** Notices name the missing person, often their townland or county of origin in Ireland, the name of the person posting the notice, and approximate dates.
- **Why essential for 1840s:** Many Famine-era arrivals used this column to locate family members. Even if your ancestor is not the subject of a notice, a sibling or spouse may be — which can unlock a precise Irish origin that no passenger list would provide.
- **Source:** Wikipedia, *Boston* article.

---

### 3. Naturalization Records

- **Process in the 1840s:** Naturalization required two steps — (1) a **Declaration of Intention** ("first papers"), filed soon after arrival, and (2) a **Petition for Naturalization** ("second papers"), filed after five years' residence. Declarations are especially valuable because they often record the immigrant's birthplace, age, and date of arrival.
- **Courts that filed Boston naturalizations:** Suffolk County Superior Court, Boston Municipal Court, Suffolk County Court of Common Pleas, U.S. Circuit Court for Massachusetts, U.S. District Court for Massachusetts. Pre-1906, any court of record could naturalize.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906* (collection ID 1459213) — reported at 345,678 records, 172,839 images. Covers the 1840s window.
- **Physical holdings:** Suffolk County records (1790s–1906) are at the **Massachusetts Archives**, Columbia Point, Boston. Federal court records are at **NARA–Waltham**.
- **Additional index:** The New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS) has published indexes to many early Boston naturalization records.
- **Also on Ancestry:** Many pre-1906 Massachusetts naturalization indexes have been digitized by both FamilySearch and Ancestry.
- **Source:** FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Naturalization and Citizenship*, "Pre-1906 Naturalization" and "Boston Naturalizations" sections.

---

### 4. Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Massachusetts start:** Massachusetts was among the earliest U.S. states to require town-level vital registration — the *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* collection on FamilySearch (collection ID 2061550, reported at ~3.4 million records) covers births, marriages, and deaths registered at the town level. Boston registrations from the 1840s fall within this collection.
- **Digitization level:** **Indexed + images** on FamilySearch. Also available on Ancestry.
- **Important caveat for immigrants:** An Irish or German immigrant who arrived in 1845 will appear in vital records only for events that occurred *in Boston after arrival* — the death of a child, a marriage, etc. Events that occurred before emigration will not appear here; pursue Irish or German records for those.
- **Source:** FamilySearch collections_search result; FamilySearch Wiki.

---

### 5. Church Records

- **Dominant denomination for 1840s immigrants:** Roman Catholic (especially Irish, some German). Boston's Catholic parishes were the primary institutional home for arriving immigrants.
- **Key parishes:** Cathedral of the Holy Cross and St. Patrick's Church documented baptisms, marriages, and burials of Irish immigrants from the 1840s onward.
- **Repository:** **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** holds parish registers. Contact: 66 Brooks Drive, Braintree, MA 02184.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* (collection ID 1921657) — reported at ~987,000 records. Coverage of specific Catholic parishes varies; not all Boston Catholic registers have been digitized.
- **Other denominations:** English immigrants may appear in Anglican/Episcopal or Congregationalist records; German immigrants in Lutheran or German Reformed registers.
- **Language:** Catholic registers of the period are typically in Latin (sacramental entries) with English or Irish names.
- **Source:** FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Church Records*, "Boston Area Churches" section.

---

### 6. Federal Census Records

- **Available censuses covering this window:**
  - **1840 census** — lists head of household only; other members shown as tick marks by age/sex category. Useful for locating an immigrant family already in Boston before the Famine wave.
  - **1850 census** — first U.S. census to list every individual by name, age, sex, occupation, and **birthplace (country level)**. Identifies the immigrant's country of origin. Critical for Famine-era arrivals.
  - **1860 census** — same format; captures those who arrived in the 1840s now settled in Boston or who may have moved inland.
- **Access:** Both 1850 and 1860 are **indexed + images** on FamilySearch and Ancestry. The 1840 census is indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch.
- **Tip:** The 1850 and 1860 mortality schedules record deaths in the preceding year and may capture immigrants who died shortly after arrival.

---

### 7. Probate Records

- **Court:** Suffolk County Probate Court, Boston.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* (collection ID 1678901) — reported at ~567,000 records. Covers the 1840s.
- **Research value for immigrants:** While many recent immigrants died without significant estates, probate files can identify family members (heirs), name a specific Irish townland in a will, or establish residence and date of death. German immigrants with small businesses may appear more frequently.

---

### 8. Land Records

- **System:** Massachusetts is a state-land state (not federal land state); land records are held at the county Registry of Deeds.
- **Repository:** **Suffolk County Registry of Deeds**, 24 New Chardon Street, Boston. Records are also available online at masslandrecords.com.
- **Research value:** Most 1840s Irish Famine arrivals were laborers or domestic servants and did not acquire real property immediately. Land records become more relevant for the 1850s–1860s as immigrants established themselves economically.

---

### 9. Military Records

- **Relevant conflict:** Mexican-American War (1846–1848). Some newly arrived immigrants enlisted.
- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Many 1840s arrivals served; pension files filed decades later often contain the most detailed biographical information (birthplace, parents, marriage, children).
- **Repositories:** Service records and pension files at **NARA** (Washington, D.C.); Massachusetts state service records at the **Massachusetts Archives**. Fold3.com (subscription) indexes military records.

---

### 10. Newspapers

- ***The Boston Pilot*** (1838–present): The principal Irish Catholic newspaper. In addition to the "Missing Persons" column, it published death notices, marriage announcements, and community news for the immigrant population. Available on microfilm; some issues available through ProQuest Historical Newspapers and Newspapers.com.
- ***Boston Daily Atlas*, *Boston Post*, *Boston Herald***: General newspapers with ship arrival columns (listing vessels and sometimes passenger names), death notices, and legal notices.
- **Access:** Newspapers.com (subscription), GenealogyBank (subscription), and the **Boston Public Library** microfilm collection.

---

### 11. Cemetery Records

- **Major Catholic cemeteries serving 1840s immigrants:** Holy Cross Cemetery and later Saint Joseph Cemetery (West Roxbury) received many Irish Catholic burials.
- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave.com has partial coverage of Boston-area cemeteries.
- **Physical records:** Sexton records and burial registers at individual cemeteries and the Archdiocese of Boston Archives.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key collections for this research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891* (ID 1784312); *Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906* (ID 1459213); *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* (ID 2061550); *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* (ID 1921657); *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* (ID 1678901) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. passenger lists; Massachusetts vital records; naturalization records; federal censuses | Subscription |
| **Boston Pilot "Missing Persons"** | Irish Famine-era missing persons notices, 1831–1921 | Free — infowanted.bc.edu |
| **Mass. Land Records** | Suffolk County deeds | Free — masslandrecords.com |
| **Fold3** | Military service records, pension files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com / GenealogyBank** | *The Boston Pilot*, Boston newspapers | Subscription |
| **NARA online (Ancestry/FamilySearch)** | Customs passenger lists (RG 36) | Via Ancestry/FamilySearch |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives at Boston (NARA–Waltham)**, 380 Trapelo Rd, Waltham, MA | Original Port of Boston customs passenger lists (RG 36); federal court naturalization records | In-person; written request |
| **Massachusetts Archives**, Columbia Point, Boston | Suffolk County naturalization records (1790s–1906); state vital records; state military records | In-person; some online |
| **New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS)**, 99–101 Newbury St, Boston | Boston naturalization indexes; extensive manuscript collections; *The Pilot* indexes | In-person (members); some records online at AmericanAncestors.org |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives**, 66 Brooks Dr, Braintree | Catholic parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) | Contact archives for access |
| **Boston Public Library**, Copley Square | Newspaper microfilm; city directories; local history collections | In-person; some digital |
| **Suffolk County Registry of Deeds**, 24 New Chardon St, Boston | Land records from colonial era forward | In-person; masslandrecords.com |

---

## Records NOT Online (or Incompletely Online)

- **Catholic parish registers** for many individual Boston parishes have *not* been fully digitized. The FamilySearch Massachusetts Church Records collection has partial coverage; the Archdiocese of Boston Archives must be contacted directly for registers not yet online.
- **Customs passenger list originals** (NARA RG 36) — the FamilySearch volume (003345210) covering 1840–1850 is only 3% name-indexed. While full-text searchable, name variants and transcription errors make manual browsing of the 389-image volume advisable for thorough research.
- **Suffolk County Court of Common Pleas naturalization files** — physical originals at the Massachusetts Archives; index coverage on Ancestry/FamilySearch is not complete for all years.
- **City directories** — Boston published city directories from the 1820s onward; not fully online but available at NEHGS and Boston Public Library. Useful for tracing an immigrant year by year after arrival.
- **Almshouse / poor relief records** — Boston's Overseers of the Poor maintained records of destitute immigrants; held at the Massachusetts Archives.

---

## Known Record Issues

- **Thin 1840s passenger list content:** U.S. Customs passenger lists of the 1840s are far less informative than later immigration records. They record country of origin, not province or townland. Finding an ancestor's specific Irish county or parish often requires triangulating from naturalization declarations, church records, The Pilot, and DNA evidence.
- **Name variants:** Irish names were frequently anglicized or misspelled at the port. Gaelic names (Ó Briain → O'Brien or Bryan; Ó Murchú → Murphy) and English-language phonetic spellings both appear. Search under multiple variants.
- **No civil registration gap:** Massachusetts was unusually early in mandating town vital registration, so unlike many U.S. jurisdictions the 1840s are *not* a gap — Boston registrations from this period exist and are largely online.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with passengers lists, then pivot to naturalization.** The passenger list confirms arrival; the Declaration of Intention (filed within the first year or two) is often where you find a more specific birthplace. Search FamilySearch collection 1459213 and NARA–Waltham for Suffolk County declarations.

2. **Search The Boston Pilot Missing Persons database early.** Even one notice can break through a brick wall by naming a specific Irish townland — information you will not find anywhere else for many Famine arrivals.

3. **Use the 1850 census strategically.** It is the first to name everyone with a birthplace country. A family arrived in 1847 will show the parents' birthplace as "Ireland" and any children born in Massachusetts afterward — establishing an approximate arrival window.

4. **Check Catholic parish registers at the Archdiocese of Boston.** Church baptismal and marriage records sometimes record a godparent or witness from the same Irish townland, pointing back to the place of origin.

5. **Mine city directories for year-by-year tracking.** Boston directories from the 1840s–1860s list residents by name, occupation, and street address. Annual gaps in a directory can indicate return migration, death, or removal inland.

6. **Consider onward migration.** Many Boston arrivals did not stay. If your ancestor disappears from Boston records by the mid-1850s, search inland Massachusetts (Lowell, Worcester, Springfield) and New York or Pennsylvania for continuation of the research trail.

7. **NEHGS (AmericanAncestors.org)** is a critical subscription resource for this research — their Boston naturalization indexes, manuscript collections, and *The Pilot* database indexes are not fully duplicated elsewhere.

# Locality Research Guide
## Native American Families in Indian Territory (Oklahoma) · Mid-1800s

---

## Jurisdictional Context

During the mid-1800s, present-day Oklahoma was designated **Indian Territory** — not a state or organized territory with county governments. It was governed by a unique combination of **tribal governments** and **federal authority through the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA)**. Oklahoma did not achieve statehood until 1907.

The **Indian Removal Act of 1830** forced the relocation of the **Five Civilized Tribes** — Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek (Muscogee), and Seminole — via what became known as the Trail of Tears (1830s–1840s). These tribes established their own governments, courts, schools, and record systems in Indian Territory. Dozens of other tribes were also relocated here from across the country throughout the 19th century.

**What this means for records:** There are no county courthouse records, no civil vital registration, and no standard U.S. state administrative records for this period. The records you need were created by **three distinct systems**: tribal governments, the federal BIA, and Christian missionaries. Each must be searched independently.

---

## Primary Record Classes & Repositories

---

### 1. 🏛️ Tribal Enrollment Rolls — THE ANCHOR RECORD

**The most important record class for this research.**

Tribal rolls documented citizenship in each tribe — essential for land rights, annuity payments, and treaty benefits.

**Pre-Dawes rolls relevant to the mid-1800s** (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Oklahoma Native American Records*):

| Roll | Date | Tribe | Notes |
|------|------|-------|-------|
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | 1851 | Cherokee | Lists Cherokees who migrated west before forced removal |
| Drennen Roll | 1852 | Cherokee | Lists "Old Settlers" and emigrants together |
| BIA Annuity Rolls | 1830s–1870s | All Five Tribes | Annual household-level rolls tied to treaty payments |
| Various tribal censuses | 1850s–1890s | Tribe-specific | Each tribe conducted its own periodic censuses |

> **Important:** The famous **Dawes Rolls (1898–1914)** are *post-period* for mid-1800s research, but they are still invaluable as a **bridge record**. Each Dawes enrollment card records the applicant's age, parents, and family connections — information that reaches back into the 1840s–1870s. Always search Dawes even if your research target lived in the mid-1800s.

**FamilySearch Collection:** *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959*
- Collection ID: 1987432
- Records: 567,890 | Images: 283,945
- Access: [familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432)
- **Digitization level: Indexed + images** — searchable by name

**FamilySearch Digitized Volume:** Tribal Enrollment Cards, Indian Territory, 1840–1870
- Volume: 002298871 | 940 images
- **99% name-indexed + full-text searchable** — highest accessibility level

---

### 2. 🏛️ National Archives at Fort Worth — The Core Repository

The **National Archives at Fort Worth, TX** holds **Record Group 75 (Bureau of Indian Affairs)** — the single most important repository for Indian Territory research.

**What RG 75 contains:**
- **Annuity payment rolls (M234):** Annual rolls listing heads of household (and sometimes full families) who received treaty-stipulated payments. Compiled by BIA agents from the 1830s onward. Partially indexed on FamilySearch and Fold3.
- **Agency correspondence:** Letters between tribal leaders, BIA agents, and Washington. Often names individuals in disputes, land matters, or family affairs.
- **School records:** Enrollment records for BIA-operated and mission schools naming students and parents.
- **Census schedules:** Special Indian census schedules separate from the federal decennial census.
- **Removal records:** Documentation from the Trail of Tears migrations, including muster rolls of people relocated.

**How to access:**
- Physical records: [National Archives at Fort Worth](https://www.archives.gov/fort-worth) — appointment recommended
- Microfilm (M234, Letters Received by the OIA): Widely held by university libraries and available on Fold3
- Fold3.com has partial digitization of BIA records (subscription required)

---

### 3. 📜 Treaty Records

Federal treaties with individual tribes defined citizenship, land rights, and annuity rights — and were administered through the BIA. Treaty texts and related documentation are held at:

- **National Archives, Washington D.C.** — original treaty documents
- **[Avalon Project, Yale Law School](https://avalon.law.yale.edu/)** — treaty texts online at no cost
- **Kappler's Indian Affairs: Laws and Treaties** — standard reference, fully digitized and searchable at [digital.library.okstate.edu/kappler](https://digital.library.okstate.edu/kappler/)

Knowing which treaty applied to your ancestor's tribe and date is critical — treaty terms determined who was enrolled and what records were generated.

---

### 4. ⛪ Missionary and Church Records

Christian missionaries were among the earliest record-keepers in Indian Territory, often decades before BIA documentation was thorough.

**Denominations active in Indian Territory from the 1820s–1880s:**
- **Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian** missionaries kept registers of converts, baptisms, marriages, burials, and school enrollments (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Oklahoma Native American Records*)
- Records were kept in English and sometimes in tribal languages (Cherokee, for example, had its own written syllabary by the 1820s)

**FamilySearch Collection:** *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965*
- Collection ID: 1654321
- Records: 123,456 | Images: 61,728
- Access: [familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321)

**Other repositories:**
- **American Baptist Historical Society** (Atlanta, GA) — Cherokee and other mission records
- **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia, PA) — Indian Territory mission records
- **Oklahoma Historical Society** — holds copies of many denomination records

---

### 5. 🗺️ Land Records

**FamilySearch Collection:** *Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907*
- Collection ID: 1541872
- Records: 234,567 | Images: 117,283
- Access: [familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872)

In Indian Territory, land was held communally by tribes under treaty — not by individuals under the standard federal land patent system. Records include:
- Tribal land grant documents and permits (non-citizens required permits to live/work on tribal land)
- **Allotment records** (post-1887 Dawes Act, but lists families going back earlier)
- **Intermarried citizen permits** — documented non-tribal spouses, valuable for mixed-heritage families

The Bureau of Land Management's GLO site covers only the post-allotment period; for mid-1800s land, go to NARA Fort Worth (RG 75).

---

### 6. 🔫 Military Records

Many tribal members served in the **Civil War** (both Union and Confederate), and some tribes had their own military companies.

- **Compiled Military Service Records (CMSRs)** for Indian Home Guard units: NARA, Washington D.C. — available on Fold3
- **Confederate Cherokee, Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw units**: NARA, College Park, MD
- **Pension files** for Union service: NARA — often contain family details, birth dates, and residence history
- **Fold3.com** has indexed many of the Indian Territory Civil War records

---

### 7. 📰 Tribal Newspapers

The Cherokee Nation published the **Cherokee Phoenix** (1828–1834) and later the **Cherokee Advocate** (1844–1906). These carried names of community members, obituaries, legal notices, and council proceedings.

- Cherokee Advocate: Available at the Oklahoma Historical Society and partially digitized through [Gateway to Oklahoma History](https://gateway.okhistory.org/)

---

### 8. 📊 Federal Census Records

The standard federal decennial census **did not cover Indian Territory residents** in the same way as U.S. states in this period. However:

- **Special Indian Census Schedules** were compiled for some tribes separately from the general population schedules
- The 1860 and 1870 federal censuses have **some** Indian Territory coverage — but it is incomplete and inconsistent
- Tribal-government censuses (held at NARA Fort Worth, RG 75) supplement the gaps

FamilySearch collection *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* (above) is the best digital starting point.

---

### 9. 🏫 Freedmen Records

Under post–Civil War treaties, each of the Five Civilized Tribes was required to grant citizenship to formerly enslaved people they had held (the "Freedmen"). Freedmen were enrolled on separate tribal rolls and their families are documented in:

- **Dawes Freedmen Rolls** (1898–1914) — separate enrollment cards, indexed at FamilySearch and Ancestry
- **BIA Freedmen records** in RG 75 at NARA Fort Worth
- This is a critical record class for any family with African American and Native American mixed heritage

---

## Key Repositories — Quick Reference

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives, Fort Worth TX** | RG 75 (BIA) — annuity rolls, agency records, school records, tribal censuses | [archives.gov/fort-worth](https://www.archives.gov/fort-worth) |
| **FamilySearch** | Tribal rolls 1851–1959; land records 1834–1907; mission records 1820–1965; digitized enrollment volumes | Free at familysearch.org |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** | Tribal newspapers, mission records, territorial documents | [okhistory.org](https://www.okhistory.org) |
| **Fold3** | BIA microfilm (M234), Civil War Indian units, some pension files | Subscription |
| **Ancestry** | Dawes Rolls, federal censuses, some BIA records | Subscription |
| **Tribal archives** | Each of the Five Tribes maintains its own archives (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, Seminole Nation headquarters) | Contact tribes directly |
| **OSU Library (Kappler's)** | All federal Indian treaties, fully text-searchable | Free online |

---

## Digitization Assessment

| Record Class | Coverage | Digitization Level |
|---|---|---|
| Tribal Enrollment Rolls 1851–1959 | FamilySearch collection 1987432 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Tribal Enrollment Cards 1840–1870 | FamilySearch vol. 002298871 (940 images) | ✅ 99% indexed + full-text |
| Land Records 1834–1907 | FamilySearch collection 1541872 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Mission/Church Records 1820–1965 | FamilySearch collection 1654321 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| BIA Annuity Rolls (M234) | Fold3 (partial); NARA microfilm | ⚠️ Partial — microfilm/browse |
| Treaty documents | Kappler's (text only); NARA (originals) | ⚠️ Text searchable, no images |
| Civil War military records | Fold3 (indexed) | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Agency correspondence (RG 75) | Largely physical only at NARA FW | ❌ Physical/microfilm only |
| Tribal newspapers | OHS partial digitization | ⚠️ Browse-only online |

---

## Recommended Starting Sequence

1. **Identify the tribe** — records are organized by tribal affiliation, not by geography. You must know (or determine) which tribe your ancestor belonged to before most record classes become accessible.
2. **Search FamilySearch collection 1987432** (*Indian Censuses and Rolls*) for any roll appearances 1851 onward.
3. **Search the Dawes Roll (1898–1914)** even for mid-1800s ancestors — the enrollment card may name parents, birth year, and prior residences that carry you back decades.
4. **Request BIA annuity rolls (M234) from NARA Fort Worth** for the specific tribe and agency; these are the primary mid-century household-level records.
5. **Search mission/church records** (FamilySearch collection 1654321) for the denomination active among that tribe.
6. **Contact the tribal nation's citizenship office or archive directly** — each of the Five Civilized Tribes maintains enrollment records and may have unpublished rolls.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, "Oklahoma Native American Records" · FamilySearch Wiki, "Five Civilized Tribes Records" · FamilySearch collections_search (Oklahoma, 1830–1880) · FamilySearch volume_search (Indian Territory, 1840–1870) · Wikipedia, "Oklahoma"*

output.text_response:
Searching for place information and jurisdictional context for Pennsylvania in the late 1700s.Good — the standardPlace is confirmed as `Pennsylvania, United States`. Now let me run collection searches, FamilySearch wiki place pages, external links, and additional wiki searches in parallel.Now let me search for specific digitized volumes and get more details on key collections.I have enough data to compile the guide. Here is the full locality survey:

---

## Pennsylvania Quaker Records — Locality Guide
**Place:** Pennsylvania, United States | **Period:** Late 1700s (c. 1750–1810) | **Focus:** Religious meeting records and family tracing

---

### Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania was founded in 1682 by William Penn specifically as a Quaker refuge, making it the heartland of American Friends history. The state ratified the Constitution in 1787, so the late 1700s spans both late colonial and early national periods. **Pennsylvania did not mandate statewide civil registration of births and deaths until 1906** — meaning Quaker meeting records are not merely a supplement to civil records; they *are* the primary vital-records source for this period (confirmed by the FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Vital Records* and *Pennsylvania Church Records*). County marriage licenses and bonds were kept from the colonial period onward at the county courthouse.

The counties with the densest Quaker populations in the late 1700s were **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware** — start your research there unless you have reason to focus elsewhere.

---

### 1. Quaker Meeting Records ⭐ PRIMARY SOURCE

**What they contain:** Monthly meetings recorded births, marriages, deaths, disownments (disciplinary removals from membership), and certificates of removal (when a Friend moved to another meeting). Marriage certificates were signed by every person present at the ceremony — a unique feature that can document dozens of related and neighboring Quaker families at once. These record types are confirmed by the FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Church Records — Quaker (Friends) Records*.

**Record types to seek in this order:**

| Record Type | What It Tells You |
|---|---|
| **Birth/death registers** | Dates, parents, sometimes sponsors |
| **Marriage records** | Couple, date, witness list (up to 50–100 names) |
| **Certificates of removal** | Origin and destination meeting, family members |
| **Disownment minutes** | Name, offense, date — confirms identity and community |
| **Monthly meeting minutes** | Broader family and community context |

**FamilySearch digitized collection:**
- **"Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985"** (Collection ID 1401638) — 1,245,830 records / 982,415 persons / 607,220 images — **indexed + images**. This is the broadest entry point and includes Quaker meeting registers alongside other denominations. → https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638

**FamilySearch digitized volume:**
- **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800** (Image Group 001188322) — 145 images — ⚠️ **Browse-only: 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** You must page through images one by one. This volume covers the governing body of Pennsylvania Quakerism during the entire target period.

**Physical and microfilm repositories (most complete coverage):**
- **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** (Swarthmore, PA) — the largest single repository of American Quaker records. Holds original monthly and quarterly meeting registers, minutes, and certificates of removal. Many collections are also available on FamilySearch microfilm. Catalog: https://www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library
- **Quaker and Special Collections, Haverford College** (Haverford, PA) — second major repository; particularly strong for Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records. Catalog: https://www.haverford.edu/quaker-and-special-collections

**Published abstracts (essential finding aid):**
- **William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes** — abstracts birth, marriage, death, removal, and disownment records from Pennsylvania monthly meetings. Volume 2 covers the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting specifically. This is the fastest way to survey whether a family appears in meeting records before committing to browsing original registers. Available in many genealogical libraries and on Archive.org. Confirmed by the FamilySearch Wiki *Pennsylvania Church Records — Published Quaker Abstracts*.

---

### 2. Marriage Records

**Quaker marriages** were performed within meeting, not by clergy, and recorded in the meeting's marriage register. The ceremony produced a certificate signed by all witnesses — a document that survives at Swarthmore and Haverford and is partially captured in Hinshaw's abstracts.

**Civil/county records** (for non-Quaker neighbors or Quakers who married outside meeting):
- **"Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950"** (Collection 1571957) — 312,045 records / 156,023 images — **indexed + images**. → https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957
- **"Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900"** (Collection 1502909) — 198,765 records / no images — **name index only** (no images attached). → https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909

Note: If a Quaker married outside meeting, the monthly meeting often recorded a disownment for "marrying out" — itself a useful record if the marriage register is missing.

---

### 3. Probate Records

Quakers were known for writing detailed wills, often naming all children and grandchildren. Probate was filed at the **county Register of Wills** from the colonial period onward. This is especially valuable for the late 1700s since it predates civil registration.

- **"Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994"** (Collection 1417683) — 893,214 records / 446,607 images — **indexed + images**. Covers wills, administrations, inventories, and guardianship bonds. → https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683

Inventories can name household members and reveal the economic context of the family. Guardianship bonds sometimes identify minor children and surviving parents.

---

### 4. Tax Records (Pre-1790 Census Substitute)

There was no federal census before 1790. For the 1750s–1780s, **county tax assessment lists** are the best substitute for locating Quaker households.

- Pennsylvania tax records (assessment lists and tax duplicates) survive from the **1690s** in some counties — the 1693 Chester County tax list is among the earliest (confirmed by *Pennsylvania Land and Probate Records* wiki article).
- Tax lists can identify a Quaker household's county, township, and approximate economic standing.
- Many are held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) and county historical societies; some are available on microfilm through FamilySearch.

The **1790 federal census** is the first available enumeration — accessible on FamilySearch and Ancestry. It lists heads of household only, so combine it with meeting records to reconstruct full families.

---

### 5. Land Records

- **Pennsylvania Land Office** (Harrisburg): warrants, surveys, and patents begin with Penn's original grants in 1681. These predate county formation and are essential for placing a Quaker family on the landscape. Available through the Pennsylvania State Archives.
- **County deed books**: held at county courthouses; start dates vary by county formation.
- Land transfers within Quaker families sometimes appear in meeting records as well, since the community monitored economic dealings.

---

### 6. Military Records — Special Note for Quakers

The Religious Society of Friends maintained a **pacifist testimony** and members were expected to refuse military service. During the Revolutionary War (1775–1783), many Pennsylvania Quakers who took up arms or otherwise supported the war effort were **disowned by their monthly meeting**. Those disownment records are genealogically valuable: they confirm the individual's identity, meeting affiliation, and approximate date of departure from the community.

If an ancestor is absent from later Quaker meeting records, check disownment minutes for the Revolutionary War years — then look for them in civil or other church records after they left the meeting.

Standard military records (pension files, service records) at NARA are unlikely to contain Quaker men from this period, but may document non-Quaker neighbors whose records intersect with the family.

---

### 7. Newspaper and Published Sources

- **Historical newspapers** (Newspapers.com, Chronicling America at the Library of Congress) occasionally published Quaker death notices, particularly for prominent Philadelphia Friends.
- **Pennsylvania Gazette** (1728–1800) and other colonial-era Philadelphia papers are searchable on Accessible Archives and Readex/America's Historical Newspapers.

---

### Access Summary by Digitization Level

| Record Type | Digitization Level | Where to Access |
|---|---|---|
| Quaker meeting registers (broad) | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch Collection 1401638 |
| Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes 1750–1800 | ⚠️ **Browse-only** (0% indexed) | FamilySearch Image Group 001188322 |
| Probate records | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch Collection 1417683 |
| Civil/county marriages | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch Collection 1571957 |
| Compiled marriages index | **Index only** (no images) | FamilySearch Collection 1502909 |
| Hinshaw abstracts | **Printed finding aid** | Libraries, Archive.org |
| Original monthly meeting registers | **Physical/microfilm** | Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore); Haverford Quaker Collections |
| Tax assessment lists (pre-1790) | **Physical/microfilm** | PA State Archives; county historical societies |
| Pennsylvania Land Office warrants | **Physical** | PA State Archives, Harrisburg |

---

### Recommended Research Order

1. **Search Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia*** first to identify which monthly meeting(s) your family belonged to and what events were recorded. This tells you exactly which meeting registers to request.
2. **Search FamilySearch Collection 1401638** (Church and Town Records) — indexed, so name-searchable.
3. **Browse the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes** (Image Group 001188322) if your family was in that meeting's geographic orbit — remember it requires image-by-image browsing.
4. **Request original registers** from Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore) or Haverford for the specific monthly meeting.
5. **Cross-check with probate** (Collection 1417683) for wills naming children.
6. **Pull tax lists** for the 1750s–1780s to locate the family before 1790.
7. **Check disownment minutes** if a family member disappears from meeting records around the Revolutionary War years.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki — Pennsylvania Church Records (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Church_Records); FamilySearch Wiki — Pennsylvania Vital Records; FamilySearch Wiki — Pennsylvania Land Records; FamilySearch collections and volume search results.*

---

# Locality Guide: Pittsburgh / Allegheny County, Pennsylvania (c. 1865–1906)
### Focus: Steel Industry & Occupational Sources

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Formed** | Allegheny County created 1788 from Washington and Westmoreland Counties |
| **County seat** | Pittsburgh (established 1758; incorporated as city 1816) |
| **Parent jurisdiction** | Pennsylvania (state-land state — metes-and-bounds survey system) |
| **Economy** | Iron and steel manufacturing, coal mining, glass, coke production; Carnegie Steel and dozens of allied firms dominated by the 1880s |
| **Ethnic composition** | Irish, German (pre–Civil War); massive post-1870 "new immigration" of Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian, and Eastern European Jewish workers to the mills |
| **Religious denominations** | Roman Catholic (numerous ethnic parishes), Presbyterian, Episcopal, Methodist, Lutheran, Greek Catholic / Byzantine, Jewish congregations |
| **Key events** | Great Railroad Strike of 1877 (Pittsburgh center), Homestead Strike 1892, Johnstown Flood 1889 (regional), Spanish-American War 1898 |

**Source:** FamilySearch Wiki, *Allegheny County, Pennsylvania Genealogy*; Wikipedia, *Pittsburgh*

---

## Boundary Changes

- **Stable throughout the period.** Allegheny County's borders did not change between 1865 and 1906; all Pittsburgh records belong squarely to Allegheny County.
- The independent city of **Allegheny** (across the river from Pittsburgh) was a separate municipality until annexation in 1907 — if ancestors lived in Allegheny City, its records were created under that city's administration, though both fall within Allegheny County's court jurisdiction. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, Allegheny County Genealogy)*

---

## Available Record Types

### ⭐ Vital Records (Civil Registration)
| | |
|---|---|
| **Births & Deaths** | Allegheny County recorded births and deaths from **1870** — earlier than Pennsylvania's statewide mandate (which did not begin until **1906**). Pre-1870 vital events must be sought in church registers. |
| **Marriages** | Marriage licenses held at the **Allegheny County Courthouse** from **1885**. Earlier marriages were recorded at the county level from the colonial period; older marriage bonds and licenses are held at the courthouse and state archives. |
| **Where held** | Allegheny County Courthouse (Register of Wills / Recorder of Deeds); Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg |
| **Online** | **FamilySearch: Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950** — 524,189 indexed records with images (**Indexed + images**). **FamilySearch: Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950** — 312,045 indexed records with partial image coverage (**Indexed + images**). **FamilySearch: Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900** — 198,765 index entries, **no images** (**Index only — no images**). |
| **Death Certificates** | The major FamilySearch death certificate collection — **Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968** (4,218,763 fully indexed records with images) — begins just *after* the late-1800s window but is essential for workers who died after 1906; it names occupation, employer, birthplace, and parents' birthplaces. |
| **Gap** | Allegheny County's own 1870–1905 birth and death registers do not appear as a digitized, name-searchable FamilySearch collection in survey results. These records exist physically at the **county courthouse and Pennsylvania State Archives** and should be requested directly or consulted on microfilm. |

*(Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, Pennsylvania Vital Records; FamilySearch collections_search)*

---

### Church Records — **Critical for this period**

Because civil registration was incomplete before 1906, **ethnic parish registers are the primary source** for baptisms, marriages, and burials of immigrant steel workers.

| Denomination | Communities served | Records | Where held |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roman Catholic (Irish/German parishes) | Earlier immigrant families | Baptisms, marriages, burials from 1840s | Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives; originals at parishes |
| Roman Catholic (Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian parishes) | Post-1870 "new immigrant" mill workers | Baptisms, marriages, burials; often in Polish, Slovak, Croatian, or Latin | Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives; many parishes retain originals |
| Greek Catholic / Byzantine | Slovak and Carpatho-Rusyn workers | Baptisms, marriages, burials | Individual parishes; Diocese of Passaic or Pittsburgh Byzantine archives |
| Presbyterian, Episcopal, Methodist | Older Scottish/English/German families | Baptisms/marriages/burials | Individual churches; Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia) |
| Jewish congregations | Eastern European Jewish workers | Vital events | Congregation archives; Jewish community center records |

**Online:** **FamilySearch: Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** — 1,245,830 records with approximately 607,220 images (**Indexed + partial images**). Coverage is uneven by congregation; check the collection for specific Allegheny County parishes.

**Language note:** Slovak, Polish, Croatian, and Latin records require the researcher to read or translate those languages. Many pre-1906 baptismal entries are entirely in Latin.

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, Allegheny County Genealogy — Vital and Church Records)*

---

### Census Records

| Census | Value for steel-era research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1870 Federal | First census after the industry boom; records occupation | **Indexed + images** — Ancestry, FamilySearch, MyHeritage |
| 1880 Federal | Records occupation, relationship to head, birthplace of both parents | **Indexed + images** — Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| 1890 Federal | **Largely destroyed** by fire (1921) — a critical gap | No substitute for Allegheny County; use 1889–1890 city directories instead |
| 1900 Federal | Records immigration year, years in U.S., naturalization status, number of children | **Indexed + images** — Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| Mortality Schedules 1850–1885 | Records deaths in year prior to each census — cause of death, occupation | Available on Ancestry and FamilySearch; valuable for industrial accident deaths |

**Access:** U.S. Federal Census — Ancestry.com (subscription); FamilySearch (free).

**Note:** The destroyed 1890 census is a major gap for industrial families at the height of the steel boom. City directories, tax records, and church registers from 1889–1891 should be used as substitutes.

---

### ⭐⭐ Industrial & Occupational Records — **Specialty focus for this guide**

This category is the defining feature of Pittsburgh research in this era and requires looking well beyond standard online databases.

| Source | What it contains | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Company employment records** | Hire dates, departments, pay rates, nationalities — *fragmentary survival* | **Heinz History Center** (Western Pennsylvania industrial collections); **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area archives** | Physical — in-person or written inquiry |
| **Pittsburgh city directories** | Annual listing of residents with occupation and street address; begin **1813** | **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** (full run); also on Ancestry.com (partial) | Physical (full run); Ancestry (partial — **Subscription**) |
| **Union membership records** | Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers (est. 1876); local lodge rolls, grievance files | **Historical Society of Western Pennsylvania / Heinz History Center** | Physical |
| **State factory inspector reports** | Annual reports naming mills, employee counts, accident records; published 1889+ | **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg; Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh | Physical; some digitized at HathiTrust |
| **Federal census occupation columns** | 1870, 1880, 1900 enumerate occupation for every adult male; 1880 adds employer name in some schedules | Ancestry, FamilySearch | **Indexed + images** |
| **NARA manufacturing census schedules** | 1870 and 1880 industry schedules record establishment names, capital, employees, wages | National Archives at Philadelphia (NARA Mid-Atlantic Region) | Physical / microfilm |

**Key repositories for industrial research:**
- **Senator John Heinz History Center**, 1212 Smallman St, Pittsburgh — the primary repository for western Pennsylvania industrial and immigration history. Holds company records, union files, ethnic community records, and a library of city directories.
- **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** — stewardship of steel-era industrial sites and some surviving company records.
- **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** — city directories from 1813 onward; the Pennsylvania Department collection.

*(Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, Allegheny County Genealogy — Industrial and Occupational Records; Wikipedia, Pittsburgh)*

---

### Immigration & Naturalization Records

Steel-era Pittsburgh was one of America's great immigrant destinations. The research trail typically runs: **passenger list → declaration of intention → petition for naturalization**.

| Record | Date range | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passenger arrival lists (Baltimore & Philadelphia ports — primary entry points) | 1820s–1906+ | NARA; Ancestry; FamilySearch | **Indexed + images** on Ancestry; FamilySearch has partial coverage |
| Declarations of Intention ("first papers") | Pre-1906: filed in any court of record | Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas records, 1798–1930, at **National Archives at Philadelphia** | Physical / microfilm |
| Petitions for Naturalization | Pre-1906: filed in any court of record | Same as above | Physical / microfilm |
| Post-1906 naturalization records | 1906+ — standardized federal forms with much richer biographical detail | NARA (federal courts); Ancestry | **Indexed + images** |

**Important note on passenger lists:** Full manifests list every person aboard — including infants and children traveling with immigrant parents. When searching for a mill worker, examine the entire manifest page for accompanying family members. Post-1890 manifests also begin recording destination cities, which helps confirm Pittsburgh-bound migrants.

**Note on pre-1906 naturalizations:** Before 1906, any court of record could naturalize immigrants. Pittsburgh steel workers may have naturalized in Allegheny County Common Pleas, U.S. District Court, or even courts in other states if they moved. The National Archives at Philadelphia holds the bulk of Allegheny County naturalizations 1798–1930.

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, Allegheny County Genealogy — Immigration Records; FamilySearch Wiki, Pennsylvania Vital Records — Court Records)*

---

### Probate & Court Records

| Type | Date range | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wills & estate administrations | From 1788 (county formation) | Allegheny County Register of Wills | **FamilySearch: Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** — 893,214 indexed records, ~446,607 images (**Indexed + partial images**) |
| Guardianship files | From 1788 | Allegheny County Orphans' Court | Physical at courthouse |
| Civil suits & equity cases | From 1788 | Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas | Physical at courthouse / PA State Archives |
| Coroner's inquest records | 1870s onward | Allegheny County Coroner | Physical — Heinz History Center holds some; courthouse holds others. **Industrial accident deaths** are frequently documented here. |

**Research tip:** Coroner's inquest records are particularly valuable for steel-era families — mill accidents, furnace explosions, and industrial injuries generated detailed inquests that name employer, witnesses, occupation, and next of kin.

---

### Land Records

Pennsylvania is a **state-land (metes-and-bounds) state.** Deeds and surveys describe land by reference to natural landmarks and neighbors, not a township grid.

| Type | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|
| County deed books | Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds (from 1788) | Physical at courthouse; some volumes microfilmed |
| Tax assessment lists | Allegheny County / Pennsylvania State Archives | Physical; partial FamilySearch microfilm |
| Pennsylvania warrant/survey/patent records | Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg (Land Office) | Physical; BLM GLO not applicable (state-land state) |

**Note:** Mill workers who rented rather than owned property will not appear in deed records, but **tax assessment lists** record all taxable residents (including renters of personal property) and serve as a useful annual census substitute, especially to bridge the lost 1890 census.

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, Pennsylvania Land and Probate Records)*

---

### Military Records

Two conflicts touch this period directly:

| Conflict | Record types | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Civil War (1861–1865) | Service records, pension files, bounty land warrants, regimental histories | NARA (Washington, D.C.); Pennsylvania State Archives (state adjutant general records) | Pension files: **Fold3** (subscription) / NARA request; PA rosters: Pennsylvania State Archives |
| Spanish-American War (1898) | Service records, pension files | NARA | **Fold3** (subscription) |
| Civil War veterans schedules | 1890 Union Veterans Schedule (partial survivor of the 1890 fire) | NARA / Ancestry | **Indexed + images** on Ancestry — check for Allegheny County entries |

**Note:** Many steel workers who were Civil War veterans appear in pension files with rich biographical detail — age, physical description, residence, occupation, and family information. Pension files often span decades and are among the most informative records of the period.

---

### Cemetery Records

| Cemetery | Denomination/type | Online coverage |
|---|---|---|
| Calvary Catholic Cemetery, Pittsburgh | Roman Catholic | FindAGrave (partial) |
| Allegheny Cemetery, Lawrenceville | Non-sectarian (oldest major cemetery, est. 1844) | FindAGrave (substantial); sexton records at cemetery office |
| St. Mary's Cemetery and other ethnic Catholic cemeteries | Polish, Slovak, Croatian Catholic communities | FindAGrave (variable) |
| Various Protestant churchyard cemeteries | Presbyterian, Methodist, Episcopal | FindAGrave |

**Access:** **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com — free) is the primary online resource. Physical sexton records (burial registers) are held at individual cemetery offices and vary in completeness. The Heinz History Center holds some cemetery record transcriptions.

---

### Newspaper Records

Pittsburgh had a vigorous press in the late 1800s. Obituaries, accident reports, strike coverage, and legal notices are all genealogically significant.

| Paper | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|
| *Pittsburgh Gazette* (later *Pittsburgh Post-Gazette*) | Major daily; covered industrial accidents, strikes, labor news, obituaries | **Newspapers.com** (subscription); Carnegie Library microfilm |
| *Pittsburgh Dispatch* | Major daily through the 1890s | Carnegie Library microfilm |
| Ethnic-language press | Slovak, Polish, Croatian-language papers serving mill worker communities | Heinz History Center; some on microfilm at Carnegie Library |

**Research tip:** Industrial accident reports in Pittsburgh newspapers from the 1880s–1900s routinely named the injured or killed worker, their nationality, employer, mill location, and sometimes their neighborhood. These are invaluable when death certificates don't yet exist.

---

### Tax Records

- **Allegheny County tax assessment lists** (annual) survive from the county's 1788 formation and are held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** and partially at the courthouse.
- These records are **physical/microfilm only** — not available as a searchable online collection.
- They are the best substitute for the lost 1890 census — searching 1889, 1890, and 1891 lists bridges that gap.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key collections for this place & period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records 1708–1985; Pennsylvania, County Marriages 1885–1950; Pennsylvania, Probate Records 1683–1994; Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages 1677–1950; U.S. Federal Census 1870–1900 | Free |
| **Ancestry** (ancestry.com) | U.S. Federal Census collection; Pittsburgh city directories (partial); Pennsylvania naturalization records; passenger lists | Subscription |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Civil War service records and pension files; Spanish-American War records | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery inscriptions for Allegheny County cemeteries | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Pittsburgh Gazette / Post-Gazette; Pittsburgh Dispatch; regional papers | Subscription |
| **NARA Online** (archives.gov) | Federal census schedules; post-1906 naturalizations; passenger lists (Ancestry partnership) | Free/subscription |
| **HathiTrust** (hathitrust.org) | Pennsylvania factory inspector reports (some years); Pennsylvania state publications | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Senator John Heinz History Center**, Pittsburgh | Industrial company records; union archives; immigration records; Pittsburgh city directories; ethnic community collections | In-person; contact for remote research inquiries |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** — Pennsylvania Dept. | City directories 1813+; local history; microfilm of Pittsburgh newspapers | In-person; some remote duplication services |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Marriage licenses (1885+); deed books; Register of Wills (wills, estates) | In-person; Recorder of Deeds also maintains some online indexes |
| **National Archives at Philadelphia** (NARA Mid-Atlantic) | Allegheny County naturalizations 1798–1930; federal court records; census microfilm | In-person; mail/duplication requests |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg | Tax assessment lists; state factory inspector records; Pennsylvania National Guard records; state-level vital records (1906+) | In-person; online catalog at phmc.pa.gov |
| **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Parish registers — baptisms, marriages, burials for Catholic ethnic parishes | Written inquiry; appointment |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society**, Philadelphia | Presbyterian church registers for western Pennsylvania | In-person; some remote services |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** | Steel industry site records; company history materials | Contact for research inquiries |

---

## Records NOT Online (Physical or Microfilm Only)

- **Allegheny County birth and death records, 1870–1905** — exist at the courthouse and Pennsylvania State Archives; not in any name-searchable online database based on this survey
- **County tax assessment lists** — physical at Pennsylvania State Archives
- **Company employment records** — fragmentary survivors at Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel; not digitized
- **Union membership rolls (Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers)** — Heinz History Center; physical only
- **Coroner's inquest records (industrial accident deaths)** — courthouse and Heinz History Center; physical only
- **Allegheny County naturalizations pre-1906** — at National Archives at Philadelphia; available on microfilm, not yet fully online
- **State factory inspector reports** (most years) — Pennsylvania State Archives; some years on HathiTrust

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 Federal Census — substantially destroyed** by the 1921 Commerce Department fire. This is a critical gap for Pittsburgh's steel boom peak. Substitutes: 1890–1891 city directories (Carnegie Library); Allegheny County tax assessment lists; church registers; state factory inspector reports.
- **No known courthouse fire** for Allegheny County — records are generally intact from 1788 onward.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the 1880 and 1900 censuses** — these two enumerations bracket the lost 1890 and together give you occupation, employer district, birthplace of both parents (1880), and immigration year and naturalization status (1900). The combination narrows ethnicity and arrival window.

2. **Follow the ethnic parish, not just the county.** A Slovak steelworker in Homestead or Braddock almost certainly baptized his children and buried his dead at his national parish (often St. Michael's, St. Mary Magdalene, or similar). The **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** is the key repository.

3. **City directories fill the 1890 gap and more.** Pittsburgh directories (Carnegie Library, 1813+; Ancestry, partial) list occupation and address annually. Cross-reference multiple years to track a worker's movements between mill towns (Homestead, Braddock, McKeesport, Duquesne were all part of the steel corridor).

4. **The Heinz History Center is the crown jewel** for industrial-era Pittsburgh research. Its Western Pennsylvania Genealogical Society collection and industrial archives hold materials unavailable anywhere else. Plan an in-person visit or contact their genealogy volunteers.

5. **Naturalization timing matters.** Many immigrant workers delayed naturalization for years. A worker who arrived in 1885 may not have naturalized until 1895 or 1905. Search the Allegheny County naturalizations at the National Archives at Philadelphia across the full span of a worker's likely U.S. residence.

6. **Check coroner's inquests for industrial accidents.** Mill deaths were common. An inquest file names the decedent, employer, foreman, witnesses, cause of death, and next of kin — often providing family detail unavailable in any church or vital record.

7. **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records on FamilySearch** is worth searching broadly — it aggregates records from many Allegheny County congregations across the 1708–1985 window and includes some records not easily found elsewhere.

8. **The 1906 death certificate collection is your safety net** for workers who lived into the early 20th century. These certificates state occupation, employer, birthplace, and parents' birthplaces — information that can anchor searches backward into the 1870s–1890s.

9. **Spelling variations are critical** for immigrant surnames. Slovak, Polish, Croatian, and Italian names were frequently anglicized, abbreviated, or phonetically respelled by census enumerators and church clerks. Search broadly on phonetic variants and given-name equivalents (e.g., Jozef / Joseph / Joe).

10. **FamilySearch's compiled marriages collection (1700–1900)** has 198,765 index entries but **no images** — it is an index only. Always follow up with the original marriage bond or license at the county courthouse when an entry is found here.

# Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — Locality Research Guide
## Tracing Families Affected by the 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic

---

### Jurisdictional Context

Philadelphia City and Philadelphia County (both established 1682; essentially coterminous by the 1790s) were the center of the epidemic. In 1793, Philadelphia was the U.S. capital, with a population of approximately **50,000**. The epidemic struck **August–November 1793**, killing roughly **5,000 people — one-tenth of the city's population** — while thousands more fled (Wikipedia, *Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793*). Records of the dead and relief efforts are held by the Philadelphia City Archives, the Historical Society of Pennsylvania, and various church archives.

> ⚠️ **Critical gap:** Pennsylvania had **no statewide civil registration** of births, marriages, or deaths in 1793 — that did not begin until 1906. FamilySearch's "Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968" post-dates the epidemic by 113 years and is **not applicable**. All vital events from 1793 must be reconstructed from church registers, published accounts, committee minutes, and bills of mortality.

---

### 1. Epidemic-Specific Sources *(Highest Priority)*

These sources are unique to the outbreak and have no standard genealogical equivalent.

#### A. Mathew Carey's Published Account
*A Short Account of the Malignant Fever Lately Prevalent in Philadelphia* (1793) contains **named lists of the dead** and statistical tallies. Multiple editions appeared in 1793–1794. Digitized copies are freely available on the **Internet Archive** (archive.org) and Google Books. This is a manually searchable text — no name index exists online.

#### B. Committee Minutes and Reports
The citizen relief committee, chaired by Mayor Matthew Clarkson, documented the sick, the dead, and aid recipients during the outbreak. Its **minutes and reports** are held as manuscript collections at the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**. The FamilySearch wiki article *Philadelphia Genealogy* (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Philadelphia_Genealogy#Yellow_Fever_1793) cites these as primary manuscript sources for the epidemic.

#### C. Bush Hill Hospital Records
Bush Hill was the emergency fever hospital established by the Committee. Patient records are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and/or **HSP**. Physical/in-person access only; no digitized version is confirmed.

#### D. Philadelphia Bills of Mortality / City Death Registers
The City of Philadelphia maintained burial tallies during the epidemic. These are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives**, 3101 Market St., Philadelphia, PA. Physical access required; not confirmed digitized on FamilySearch.

---

### 2. Church Burial Records *(Essential — Primary Vital Source)*

Because no civil death registration existed, **church burial registers are the most important genealogical record type** for 1793 deaths. The FamilySearch wiki (*Philadelphia Genealogy — Church Records* section) identifies church records as the primary source of vital information before 1906, noting that Philadelphia's diverse religious communities each maintained registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials.

Key congregations with active 1793 registers:
- **Christ Church** (Anglican/Episcopal) — one of the city's largest; registers widely transcribed
- **Gloria Dei / Old Swedes' Church** (Lutheran/Episcopal) — one of the oldest congregations in Pennsylvania
- Quaker Monthly Meeting records, Methodist, Presbyterian, Catholic (St. Mary's), Lutheran, Baptist

**FamilySearch digitized collection:**
> **"Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985"** — Collection #1401638
> 1,245,830 records | 982,415 persons indexed | 607,220 images
> https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638

This is the primary FamilySearch entry point for 1793 church burials. Note: image count (607,220) is lower than record count (1,245,830), indicating **partial image coverage** — some entries are name-indexed without attached images; follow up at original repositories for those.

The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** has compiled additional indexes to Philadelphia church records not fully represented on FamilySearch. Many colonial and early national registers have been transcribed and published in the GSP's *Publications* series.

---

### 3. Census Records

| Census | Date | Coverage | Access |
|--------|------|----------|--------|
| **1790 Federal Census** | 1790 | Pre-epidemic household snapshot | Indexed on FamilySearch (free) |
| **1800 Federal Census** | 1800 | Post-epidemic; shows household losses | Indexed on FamilySearch (free) |

Comparing the same household across 1790 and 1800 can reveal families that disappeared — potential evidence of epidemic deaths or flight from the city. **Mortality schedules** did not begin until 1850 and do not reach back to 1793.

---

### 4. Probate Records

The epidemic triggered a surge in wills and estate administrations in fall 1793. The **Philadelphia Register of Wills** holds original county probate files.

**FamilySearch digitized collection:**
> **"Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994"** — Collection #1417683
> 893,214 records | 893,214 persons indexed (fully indexed) | 446,607 images
> https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683

Note: image count is approximately half the record count — many index entries lack attached images. Probate files from 1793 may require a follow-up visit to the Philadelphia Register of Wills for images not yet online. Files may include wills, estate inventories, and letters of administration naming surviving family members.

---

### 5. Marriage Records

No civil marriage registration existed in Pennsylvania in 1793; marriages appear in church registers and Quaker meeting records.

**FamilySearch collections:**
> **"Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950"** — Collection #1571957
> 312,045 records | 156,023 images (partial image coverage)
> https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957

> **"Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900"** — Collection #1502909
> 198,765 records | **0 images — index only**
> This collection provides names and dates but has no attached images; original records must be located separately.
> https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909

---

### 6. Land and Tax Records

Philadelphia tax lists document householders by year and can establish who was present before and after the epidemic. Pennsylvania county tax records for Philadelphia County are held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) and the **Philadelphia City Archives**. FamilySearch has microfilmed some Pennsylvania tax lists — search the **FamilySearch Catalog** under "Philadelphia County — Taxation" for browse-only volumes.

---

### 7. Newspapers *(High Value for This Research)*

Philadelphia newspapers published during the epidemic documented **obituaries, subscriber death notices, named lists of the dead, and official bulletins** from the Committee. Key titles active in 1793:
- *Pennsylvania Gazette*
- *Federal Gazette*
- *General Advertiser* (later *Aurora General Advertiser*)

**Newspapers.com** (https://www.newspapers.com) — a curated external resource returned by FamilySearch — holds historical Philadelphia newspapers. **Readex Early American Newspapers** (available at many research libraries) provides deeper coverage of 1793 issues. The **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** holds bound originals.

---

### 8. Naturalization Records

Philadelphia was the nation's leading port in 1793, and pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in any court of record. Courts active in Philadelphia included the Court of Common Pleas, the Pennsylvania Supreme Court, and the U.S. District Court.

FamilySearch holds some Pennsylvania naturalization records — search the **FamilySearch Catalog** under "Philadelphia County — Naturalization and Citizenship." The **National Archives (NARA)** Philadelphia field office holds federal court records. NARA's main site (https://www.archives.gov) is a curated external link returned by FamilySearch for Pennsylvania.

---

### 9. Military Records

**Fold3** (https://www.fold3.com) and **NARA** (https://www.archives.gov) — both returned as curated external links by FamilySearch — hold Revolutionary War service records, pension files, and bounty land warrants. Many Philadelphia families in 1793 had Revolutionary War veterans; pension files often contain family depositions that name spouses, children, and dates of death that can corroborate epidemic losses.

---

### 10. Cemetery Records

**Find A Grave** (https://www.findagrave.com) — a curated FamilySearch external resource — has transcriptions and photographs from Philadelphia-area cemeteries including Christ Church Burial Ground and other colonial-era sites. Useful for confirming burial locations and finding death dates when church registers are incomplete or lost.

---

### Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**, Philadelphia | Committee minutes, Bush Hill records, newspapers, manuscript collections | In-person; some via HSP Digital Library |
| **Philadelphia City Archives**, 3101 Market St. | Bills of mortality, city death registers, municipal records | In-person |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg | Tax lists, state records, some military records | In-person + partial digitization |
| **Philadelphia Register of Wills** | Original probate files from 1793 | In-person |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** | Church record indexes, published transcriptions | Members' library |
| **FamilySearch** (free, online) | Church & Town Records #1401638 · Probate Records #1417683 · Civil Marriages #1571957 · Compiled Marriages #1502909 | familysearch.org |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Federal court naturalizations, military records | In-person + Fold3/Ancestry |
| **Internet Archive / Google Books** | Mathew Carey's published account (digitized) | Free online |

---

### Recommended Research Sequence

1. **Epidemic sources first** — Search Mathew Carey's published account (Internet Archive) for the name. Then contact HSP about the Committee minutes and Bush Hill hospital records.
2. **Church burial records** — Run the name through FamilySearch Collection #1401638 ("Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records"). For churches not yet digitized, consult the GSP's published indexes.
3. **Probate records** — Search FamilySearch Collection #1417683 ("Pennsylvania, Probate Records") for wills or administrations filed in fall 1793 or 1794.
4. **Compare 1790 and 1800 censuses** — Identify the household in 1790, then check 1800 for absences.
5. **Philadelphia newspapers, Aug–Nov 1793** — Search Newspapers.com or Readex Early American Newspapers for death notices and named lists published during the epidemic.
6. **Cemetery records** — Cross-check Find A Grave for burial confirmation.

> **Sources:** FamilySearch wiki *Philadelphia Genealogy* (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Philadelphia_Genealogy); Wikipedia *Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793* (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yellow_fever_epidemic_of_1793); FamilySearch collections search results for Pennsylvania.
