# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide
## Oklahoma Territory — Land Run of 1889 and Early Settlement (1889–1907)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

**Critical distinction:** The Oklahoma you're researching is actually **two separate jurisdictions** with entirely different record systems:

| Jurisdiction | Geography | Period | Record Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Territory** | Western OK — the Unassigned Lands opened by Land Runs | 1890–1907 | Territorial courts, county offices, federal GLO |
| **Indian Territory** | Eastern OK — Five Civilized Tribes lands | Pre-1907 | Tribal governments, Dawes Commission |

The Land Run of 1889 (April 22) opened the **Unassigned Lands** specifically. Oklahoma Territory was formally established under the **Organic Act of May 2, 1890** with Guthrie as capital. Both territories merged into the **State of Oklahoma on November 16, 1907**. Subsequent land runs opened additional areas in 1891, 1892, 1893, and 1895.

Records created before 1890 fall under prior federal administration (General Land Office, Indian Affairs). Records from 1890–1907 are territorial. Records from 1907 onward are state records. **You must work all three layers for families who arrived in 1889.**

> **Note:** FamilySearch's place system recognizes "Oklahoma, United States" beginning at statehood (+1907). For the territorial period, records appear under Oklahoma Territory or Indian Territory headers in various repositories — both feed into modern Oklahoma collections.

---

## ⭐ THE CROWN JEWEL: Land and Homestead Records

These are the **single most important source** for Land Run families and should be your first stop.

### Homestead Entry Files — National Archives (NARA, Washington D.C.)
- **Record Group 49** — General Land Office records
- Each file typically contains: original homestead application, proof of settlement with **witness statements naming neighbors**, final patent
- **Rejected applications also survive** and can contain detailed family information
- Land offices for the 1889 Run were located at **Guthrie** and **Kingfisher**
- Source: FamilySearch Wiki, *Oklahoma Land Records — Land Run and Homestead Records*

### BLM General Land Office Records *(glorecords.blm.gov)*
- Free online search for land patents
- Partially indexed on FamilySearch as well

### FamilySearch Collection — Indexed + Images
> **Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907**
> Collection ID 1541872 · 234,567 records · 117,283 images
> 🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872

Covers patents, grants, and homestead-related land records across the territorial period. Date range overlaps the entire Land Run era.

---

## Census Records

### 1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census ⚠️ RARE SURVIVOR
- The 1890 federal census was **largely destroyed** nationally (fire + water damage)
- However, the **Oklahoma Territory special census of 1890 is one of the few surviving 1890 enumerations** in the entire country — an extraordinary resource
- Held at the **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City)
- Documents early Land Run settlers just one year after the Run
- Source: FamilySearch Wiki, *Oklahoma Territory Records — Territorial Government Records* and *Oklahoma Land Records*

### 1900 and 1910 Federal Censuses
- Both cover Oklahoma and Indian Territory fully
- Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry
- The 1900 census is especially valuable — enumerates families 11 years after the Run with family relationships and birthplaces

### Indian Territory and Tribal Rolls

> **Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959**
> Collection ID 1987432 · 567,890 records · 283,945 images
> 🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432

Includes **Dawes Commission Rolls (1898–1914)**, which documented citizenship in the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, Seminole). If your ancestor lived in eastern Oklahoma (Indian Territory) rather than the Land Run zone, the Dawes Rolls are the equivalent anchor record.

### County Tax Rolls
- County-level tax rolls from the 1890s survive at the **Oklahoma Historical Society**
- Useful for locating families between census years and confirming land occupancy

---

## Vital Records — Major Gap Warning

> ⚠️ **No territorial law required registration of births or deaths.** There is no systematic vital registration for the 1889–1907 period.

- **Births/Deaths (1889–1907):** No government registration. You must use substitute sources (see Church Records, Newspapers, and Cemetery Records below).
- **Statewide vital registration** began October 1908 — but compliance was poor until the 1930s. Even early post-statehood records may be missing.
- **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) holds birth and death records from 1908 onward.

### Marriage Records — Earliest Available Civil Records

> **Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995**
> Collection ID 2134789 · 456,789 records · 913,578 persons · 228,394 images
> 🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134789

Marriage records begin with the **earliest counties (1890)** and are held at county courthouses. This is the only systematic civil vital record available for the territorial period. The 913,578 person count (vs. 456,789 records) reflects both bride and groom being indexed — these are name-searchable.

---

## Church Records

Baptist, Methodist, and Catholic congregations established roots in the new Oklahoma towns beginning in 1889. Church registers may be the **only source for births and deaths** in the territorial period.

> **Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965**
> Collection ID 1654321 · 123,456 records · 61,728 images
> 🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321

Also check directly with:
- **Oklahoma Conference of the United Methodist Church Archives** — historical parish registers
- **Diocese of Oklahoma City** — Catholic parish records
- Source: FamilySearch Wiki, *Oklahoma Vital Records — Repositories*

---

## Territorial Court Records

Oklahoma Territory district courts (1890–1907) generated rich genealogical records:
- Civil and criminal cases
- **Probate records** (wills, estate inventories)
- **Naturalization records** — many Land Run settlers were immigrants who naturalized through territorial courts; pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in any court of record

**Held at:**
- **National Archives at Fort Worth** (NARA Southwest Region) — primary repository for territorial court records
- **Oklahoma State Archives** — additional court records

Source: FamilySearch Wiki, *Oklahoma Territorial Records — Territorial Courts and Early Settlement* and *Oklahoma Territory Records — Territorial Government Records*

---

## Land Run Town Lot Records

In addition to homestead files, the 1889 Run included a race for **town lots** in new municipalities like Guthrie and Oklahoma City. Town lot claim files are held at the **Oklahoma Historical Society** along with other territorial-era land records.

---

## Tracing Origins (Where Did They Come From?)

Many Land Run settlers came from **Kansas, Texas, Missouri, and other border states.** FamilySearch Wiki (*Oklahoma Territorial Records*) specifically notes that tracing origins requires searching departure-state records. Your Oklahoma records may show a birthplace or prior state — use that to search:

- Kansas, Texas, and Missouri census records (1880 federal census is fully intact)
- State-of-origin land, court, and vital records
- Pension files at NARA if the ancestor was a Civil War veteran (many Run participants were)

---

## Military Records

- **Service records and pension files** at NARA — searchable via **Fold3** (https://www.fold3.com/) and FamilySearch
- Many 1889 Run participants were Civil War veterans — pension files contain extensive family details
- Spanish-American War (1898) service records also relevant for this generation

---

## Cemetery and Newspaper Records

**Cemetery Records:**
- **Find A Grave** (https://www.findagrave.com/) — transcribed and photographed headstones
- Oklahoma Historical Society holds early cemetery records
- Critical substitute for missing death registrations

**Newspapers:**
- **Newspapers.com** (https://www.newspapers.com/) — territorial-era Oklahoma newspapers began publishing immediately after the 1889 Run
- Guthrie and Oklahoma City papers from 1889 onward documented settler arrivals, land disputes, deaths, and marriages
- Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspaper collections for the territorial period

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What's There | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA Washington D.C.** | Homestead entry files (RG 49); military pension files | In-person or request copies; partial online via FamilySearch/BLM |
| **NARA at Fort Worth** | Oklahoma Territory district court records (1890–1907) | In-person or request copies |
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Land patents, free text search | Free online |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (OKC) | 1890 territorial census; land run claim files; town lot records; tax rolls; newspapers | In-person; some digitized |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (OKC) | Vital records from 1908; territorial court records | In-person; some online |
| **County Courthouses** | Marriage records from 1890; deed books | In-person; many on FamilySearch |
| **FamilySearch** | 4 indexed collections (land, Indian rolls, marriages, church records) | Free online |

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Early Land Records 1834–1907 | ✅ Indexed + images (234,567 records) | Collection 1541872 |
| Indian/Dawes Rolls 1851–1959 | ✅ Indexed + images (567,890 records) | Collection 1987432 |
| County Marriages 1890–1995 | ✅ Indexed + images (456,789 records) | Collection 2134789 |
| Mission & Church Records | ✅ Indexed + images (123,456 records) | Collection 1654321 |
| 1890 Oklahoma Territorial Census | ⚠️ Physical/microfilm at OHS | Rare survivor — check for digitization |
| Territorial Court Records | ⚠️ Physical at NARA Fort Worth & OKC | Limited online access |
| Births & Deaths 1889–1907 | ❌ No records exist | No registration law; use substitutes |

---

## Research Strategy for Land Run Families

1. **Start with homestead files** (NARA RG 49 / BLM GLO / FamilySearch Collection 1541872) — the anchor document for any Land Run family
2. **Check the 1890 Oklahoma territorial census** at the Oklahoma Historical Society — a rare window just one year after the Run
3. **Work forward through 1900 and 1910 federal censuses** on FamilySearch
4. **Search marriage records** (Collection 2134789) — the only civil vital record available from 1890
5. **Trace back to the origin state** using 1880 census and state-of-origin records
6. **Check church records** (Collection 1654321) and Find A Grave for birth/death substitutes
7. **Search newspapers** — territorial Oklahoma papers covered settler activity closely
8. **Check territorial court records** at NARA Fort Worth if the family had any legal matters (probate, naturalization, disputes)

---

*Sources: Wikipedia, "Land Run of 1889"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Oklahoma Territory Records," "Oklahoma Land Records — Land Run and Homestead Records," "Oklahoma Territorial Records — Territorial Courts and Early Settlement," "Oklahoma Vital Records — Registration History," "Oklahoma Vital Records — Repositories"; FamilySearch Collections 1541872, 1987432, 2134789, 1654321.*

# Locality Guide: Alabama Region, circa 1780–1820 (Pre-Statehood)

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

The territory that became Alabama did not exist as a single administrative unit in 1800 — it passed through several overlapping jurisdictions in the decades around that date:

| Period | Governing authority |
|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French colony (Mobile Bay area) |
| 1763–1780 | British West Florida |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish West Florida (Mobile area) |
| 1798–1817 | **Mississippi Territory** (U.S. organized territory) |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory (split from Mississippi Territory) |
| 14 Dec 1819 | Alabama admitted as 22nd state |

Around 1800, the vast interior of what is now Alabama had essentially no organized county government. The **first county formed in present-day Alabama** was Washington County (1800, carved from the Mississippi Territory), centered on the Tombigbee River settlements. The Mobile Bay coast remained under **Spanish West Florida** control until 1813. Records therefore exist under three or four different governing bodies simultaneously depending on exactly where in the region your ancestors lived.

- **Economy:** Subsistence agriculture, river-borne trade (Tombigbee, Alabama, Tennessee rivers), limited cotton production taking hold by 1810s
- **Ethnic composition:** Anglo-American settlers from the Carolinas, Virginia, and Georgia (surging especially post–Creek War 1813–14); French Creole community in Mobile; Spanish subjects in Mobile; enslaved African-American population; Creek, Cherokee, Choctaw, and Chickasaw nations across the interior
- **Religious denominations:** Methodist, Baptist (frontier circuit riders); Catholic (Mobile area, under Spanish then U.S. control); small number of Presbyterian congregations
- **Key events:** Creek War of 1813–14 (dramatically accelerated Anglo settlement of the interior); War of 1812 (Battle of Mobile, 1813); opening of the Tennessee Valley to settlement ~1809–1816

---

## Boundary Changes and Jurisdictional Continuity

This period involves layered, sequential jurisdictions — treat them as **one continuous research trail** rather than separate archives:

- **Before 1798 (Mobile area):** Records created under French, British, and Spanish authority. These are colonial-era foreign-power records, not U.S. territorial records.
- **1798–1817:** The entire present-day state was part of the **Mississippi Territory**. Records from this period are filed under Mississippi Territory / Mississippi, not Alabama. The FamilySearch `collections_search` for "Mississippi Territory" resolved to Mississippi-scope and surfaced four collections starting as early as 1792–1798 that cover this window.
- **1817–1819:** The Alabama Territory was organized; records begin appearing under Alabama place names.
- **Post-1819 county records** for counties formed before statehood (Washington 1800, Baldwin 1809, Madison 1809, Montgomery 1816, etc.) begin their continuous series in those years and run into the early Alabama state period — treat pre- and post-statehood county records as a single unbroken set.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Alabama did **not** have civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths during the territorial period. Statewide civil registration of vital events was not required until **1908**.
- **What exists for 1800–1819:** **No civil birth or death records** for this period. **Marriage bonds and licenses** were recorded at the county level beginning when each county was organized; the earliest county-level marriages appear around **1809** when Baldwin and Madison counties were formed.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950* — **612,345 records, 1,224,690 persons, 306,172 images** — indexed + images. The 1809–1819 slice is a small but real subset.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church baptism, marriage, and burial registers; Spanish Catholic registers in Mobile; territorial census household lists; estate inventories naming heirs.

---

### Church Records
- **Denominations present:** Catholic (Mobile, in continuous operation from 1702 under French, British, and Spanish governance); Methodist and Baptist (frontier circuits, established ~1808–1815 in the interior)
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950* — **156,789 records, 78,394 images** — indexed + images. The 1803–1819 slice covers the tail end of Spanish Mobile and the earliest American-period churches.
- **Spanish Catholic registers (Mobile, pre-1813):** Baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Mobile) survive from the Spanish period. Originals are held at the **Archivo General de Indias, Seville**, with copies at the **Library of Congress** (per the FamilySearch Wiki, *Alabama Genealogy* article, "Colonial Records" section). Microfilm copies are also held at the **Mobile Municipal Archives**.
- **French colonial records (pre-1763):** Church and civil records are at the **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)** in Aix-en-Provence, France, with microfilm at the Mobile Municipal Archives.
- **Language note:** Pre-1813 Mobile records are in **French or Spanish**; researchers must read those languages or use a translator.

---

### Census Records and Enumerations

**A layered, complex record set — the single most important tool for this period.**

| Enumeration | Coverage | Location | Digitization level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mississippi Territory Census, 1801 | Present-day Alabama settlements | Alabama Dept. of Archives & History (ADAH) | Physical/microfilm only |
| Mississippi Territory Census, 1805 | Present-day Alabama settlements | ADAH | Physical/microfilm only |
| Mississippi Territory Census, 1808 | Present-day Alabama settlements | ADAH | Physical/microfilm only |
| U.S. Federal Census, 1810 | Entire Mississippi Territory incl. Alabama | FamilySearch / Ancestry | Indexed + images |
| Mississippi Territory Census, 1810 | Present-day Alabama settlements | ADAH | Physical/microfilm only |
| U.S. Federal Census, 1820 | First federal census under "Alabama" | FamilySearch / Ancestry | Indexed + images |

- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** *Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890* — **287,634 records, no images** — **index only, no images**. This is a name index pointing to a range of sources including tax lists and territorial enumerations but does not provide viewable images through FamilySearch.
- **Mississippi-side collection:** *Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890* — **198,340 records, no images** — index only. Covers the Mississippi Territory period relevant to the Alabama region.
- **Critical note:** The territorial censuses of 1801, 1805, and 1808 **are not available online** — they are held physically at ADAH in Montgomery. The 1810 federal census IS accessible online (indexed + images at FamilySearch and Ancestry). The FamilySearch wiki article *Alabama Genealogy* confirms the ADAH holdings.

---

### Land Records

**The single richest record category for this period**, especially given the near-total absence of vital records.

- **Land system:** Alabama is a **federal-land state** (public domain land distributed by the federal government, not a state-land state). This means original land patents were issued by the U.S. General Land Office (GLO), and those records are centralized.
- **BLM General Land Office Records (glorecords.blm.gov):** Federal land patents for Alabama beginning in **1803** are searchable and downloadable **free online** with images — indexed + images. This is one of the best-digitized record sets for the pre-statehood period.
- **FamilySearch collection (Mississippi Territory side):** *Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907* — **312,450 records, 156,225 images** — indexed + images. Because present-day Alabama was part of Mississippi Territory through 1817, land transactions from 1798–1817 may appear in this collection.
- **Spanish and British land grants (Mobile area, pre-1813):** Land grants issued by Spain and Britain in the Mobile District are held at the **Archivo General de Indias (Seville)** and the **Library of Congress**, with some copies at ADAH and the Mobile Municipal Archives. These are **physical only** unless microfilmed.
- **County deed books:** County-level deed recording began when each county was organized (Washington County from 1800). These are held at county courthouses and at ADAH; many are **physical only** for this early period.
- **Tax lists:** Territorial tax lists (1800s) served as de facto census substitutes and population enumerations. Listed in the FamilySearch Census Substitutes collection above.

---

### Probate and Court Records

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985* — **438,210 records, 219,105 images** — indexed + images. Date range begins 1809, coinciding with the formation of early Alabama counties. The 1809–1819 slice is covered.
- **Mississippi Territory court records:** *Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920* — **245,670 records, 122,835 images** — indexed + images. Covers the Mississippi Territory period (1799–1817) during which the Alabama region was administered.
- **Territorial court:** The Mississippi Territory had a superior court (established 1798) with jurisdiction over the full territory, including present-day Alabama. Records from this court for the Alabama region may appear under Mississippi.
- **Physical holdings:** ADAH holds original probate and court records for the earliest Alabama counties. The county circuit courts, organized beginning 1800 (Washington County), generated deed records, estate records, and court minutes from that date.

---

### Military Records

- **Relevant conflicts:**
  - Creek War (1813–1814) — militia and volunteer service throughout the Alabama region
  - War of 1812 — service along the Gulf Coast and in the Tennessee Valley
  - Territorial militia rolls (1800s) — listed in the FamilySearch Alabama Genealogy wiki article under "Territorial Records"
- **Where held:**
  - **NARA** (Washington / Atlanta region): Federal service records and pension files for Regular Army and recognized militia service. Accessible via Fold3 (subscription) or NARA microfilm.
  - **ADAH (Montgomery):** Militia rolls for the territorial period, per the FamilySearch wiki article.
  - **Fold3.com** (subscription): War of 1812 service records, Creek War pension applications, and bounty land warrant files.
- **Pension files:** Survivors and widows of Creek War and War of 1812 veterans filed pension applications through the 1820s–1840s; these often contain detailed family genealogies. Filed at NARA; indexed at Fold3 and Ancestry.
- **Bounty land warrants:** Issued to veterans of the Creek War and War of 1812; may appear in subsequent Alabama land records when warrants were located or assigned.

---

### Tax Records

- **Importance:** For this period, **tax lists are the primary substitute for missing census records** (1801, 1805, 1808 territorial censuses are physical-only; no census between 1810 and 1820).
- **What exists:** Territorial and early county-level tax lists, cited as part of the *Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890* (287,634 records, index only) and the *Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890* (198,340 records, index only).
- **Access:** The FamilySearch index points to the original records; images are not provided through FamilySearch. Physical originals are at ADAH and individual county courthouses.
- **Note:** Tax lists from this era typically enumerate heads of household, taxable property (land, enslaved people, livestock), and sometimes free white males age 21+.

---

### Cemetery Records

- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave.com covers many Alabama cemeteries, including early family cemeteries and church graveyards, though coverage of pre-1820 burials is sparse. BillionGraves offers some additional coverage.
- **Physical records:** No sexton records exist for this period for most of Alabama. Family burial plots on private land were common and undocumented. Church graveyards in Mobile have better documentation due to the continuous Catholic institutional presence.
- **DAR transcription projects:** The Daughters of the American Revolution transcribed many early Alabama cemetery inscriptions in the early 20th century; those compilations are held at ADAH and some are available at DAR headquarters in Washington, D.C.

---

### Newspaper Records

- **Very limited for this period.** The earliest Alabama newspaper, the *Mobile Centinel*, began in 1811. Interior settlements had no local papers before ~1818.
- **Access:** Historical Alabama newspapers are available on Newspapers.com (subscription) and through the Library of Congress's Chronicling America project (free) — but coverage of 1811–1819 is fragmentary.
- **Content value for genealogy:** Legal notices (estate settlements, land sales), migration announcements, and occasionally marriage and death notices. Small settler populations meant sparse coverage.

---

### Immigration and Migration Records

- **Primary migration corridor (c. 1800–1819):** Settlers came overland from the Carolinas, Virginia, and Georgia via the Federal Road (opened 1811) and along the Natchez Trace. No formal immigration records exist for this internal U.S. migration. Research origin states (NC, SC, VA, GA) for earlier records.
- **Gulf Coast arrivals (Mobile):** Some arrivals came by sea to Mobile Bay. Spanish-era port records may exist at the Archivo General de Indias; post-1813 U.S. port arrival manifests are at NARA.
- **Naturalization:** Spanish subjects in Mobile who remained after 1813 had to naturalize under U.S. law; naturalization records from this era would be at the territorial/county court level — held at ADAH or the Mobile county courthouse.

---

## Digitized Browsable Volumes — Direct Answer to Your Question

**`volume_search` for Alabama, 1780–1820 returned zero results.** FamilySearch currently has **no catalogued image volumes** listed as browse-only or unindexed for this specific place and period. This is a digitization gap, not a records-nonexistence gap.

What this means in practice:

| Record type | What IS online | Browsable? |
|---|---|---|
| County marriages 1809+ | 306,172 images in indexed collection | Yes, via index |
| Probate records 1809+ | 219,105 images in indexed collection | Yes, via index |
| Church records 1803+ | 78,394 images in indexed collection | Yes, via index |
| Mississippi Territorial land records 1798+ | 156,225 images in indexed collection | Yes, via index |
| Mississippi court records 1799+ | 122,835 images in indexed collection | Yes, via index |
| Census/tax substitutes index | Index only, 0 images | Index only |
| BLM/GLO federal land patents | Full images at glorecords.blm.gov | Yes, browse + search |
| Spanish/French colonial records | Physical, with some microfilm | No, physical or film |
| Territorial censuses 1801/1805/1808 | Physical at ADAH | No |

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | What it holds for this place/period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Alabama: County Marriages 1809–1950 (612k records, 306k images); Probate Records 1809–1985 (438k records, 219k images); Census Substitutes Index 1810–1890 (288k records, index only); Church Records 1803–1950 (157k records, 78k images). Mississippi Territory: Territorial & State Land Records 1798–1907 (312k records, 156k images); County Court Records 1799–1920 (246k records, 123k images); Census Substitutes 1792–1890 (198k records, index only); Church Records 1800–1950 (88k records, 44k images) | Free |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents for Alabama beginning 1803 — browsable, downloadable, free | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census 1810 and 1820 (both cover the Alabama region); supplemental indexes | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | War of 1812 and Creek War service records, pension files | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) | Limited early Alabama newspaper scans | Free |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery transcriptions, variable coverage | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Alabama Department of Archives & History (ADAH), Montgomery** | Mississippi Territory censuses 1801/1805/1808/1810; territorial tax lists and militia rolls; earliest county deed books; territorial court records | In-person; some records available by mail request; [archives.alabama.gov](https://archives.alabama.gov) |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives** | Microfilm of French and Spanish colonial church records and land grants; early Mobile city records | In-person |
| **NARA Southeast Region, Atlanta, GA** | Federal records for Alabama including early court records, federal land entry case files | In-person / online request |
| **NARA, Washington, D.C.** | Military service records, pension files, War of 1812 bounty land warrants | In-person; many microfilmed |
| **Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain** | Spanish West Florida land grants, church registers, census returns for Mobile District | In-person; copies at Library of Congress |
| **Library of Congress** | Copies of Spanish colonial records; early newspapers | In-person; some digitized |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM), Aix-en-Provence, France** | French colonial records from the Mobile Bay area (pre-1763) | In-person |

---

## Records NOT Online

These record types are known to exist but are **not available in any online database**:

- **Mississippi Territory censuses of 1801, 1805, and 1808** — held physically at ADAH; not digitized
- **Spanish colonial church registers (Mobile, pre-1813)** — originals in Seville; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives; not currently online
- **Spanish colonial land grants (Mobile District)** — originals at AGI Seville; copies at Library of Congress; not fully online
- **French colonial records (pre-1763)** — at ANOM in France; not online
- **Territorial-era tax lists (images)** — indexed in FamilySearch Census Substitutes collection but images are not available through FamilySearch; originals at ADAH and county courthouses
- **Early county deed books (Washington County from 1800, etc.)** — physical at county courthouses and ADAH

---

## Known Record Losses and Gaps

- **Civil vital records:** Simply do not exist for this period. No government required registration of births or deaths before statehood (1819), and even after statehood, Alabama had no statewide civil registration until 1908. Church records and estate documents are the only substitutes.
- **Interior Alabama before ~1800:** The Cherokee, Creek, Choctaw, and Chickasaw nations controlled most of the interior. Anglo-American settlement was extremely sparse before the Creek War (1813–14). Expect very few records for the interior before ~1805.
- **Courthouse fires:** Several early Alabama counties suffered courthouse fires in the 19th century. Washington County (the earliest Alabama county, organized 1800) had partial record losses. Verify record survival for specific counties before assuming records are simply undigitized.
- **Census substitutes have no images:** Both the Alabama and Mississippi Census Substitutes collections are **index-only** — no viewable images are available through FamilySearch, limiting what you can confirm from those entries alone.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Work backward through the jurisdictional chain.** Records for someone in Alabama in 1800 are filed as Mississippi Territory records — search FamilySearch's Mississippi collections (territorial land records, court records) before concluding a record doesn't exist. Then follow the trail forward into Alabama-named collections after 1817–1819.

2. **Land records are your best proxy for everything else.** With no vital records and sparse church records outside Mobile, federal land entry files, GLO patents, and county deed books are often the only documents that place a specific person in a specific location in this period. Start at glorecords.blm.gov for federal patents, then ADAH for county deeds.

3. **Don't neglect the Mississippi Territory side.** The `collections_search` for Mississippi Territory surfaced four collections — *Territorial and State Land Records 1798–1907*, *County Court Records 1799–1920*, *Census Substitutes 1792–1890*, and *Church Records 1800–1950* — all with date ranges that overlap the pre-statehood Alabama period. These may contain records for your Alabama ancestors filed under Mississippi Territory jurisdiction.

4. **Mobile is a different research universe.** If your ancestors were in the Mobile Bay area (Washington County, Baldwin County, the coast), their pre-1813 records exist under Spanish (and before that British/French) administration — not U.S. territorial records. Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article *Alabama Genealogy*, "Colonial Records" section, for the specific archives holding those materials.

5. **Tax lists substitute for the missing 1800–1809 census gap.** No federal census covered present-day Alabama before 1810, and the 1801/1805/1808 territorial censuses are physical-only at ADAH. Territorial tax lists (in the Census Substitutes index) are the best available name-by-name listing for this gap. Request copies from ADAH directly.

6. **Pension files are genealogically rich.** Hundreds of Creek War and War of 1812 veterans and their widows who settled in Alabama filed pension applications through the 1820s–1840s, providing detailed family histories. These are at NARA and indexed at Fold3.

7. **Primary FamilySearch wiki source for this jurisdiction:** The article *Alabama Genealogy* ([familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy)) is the core starting-point guide, with sections specifically covering Territorial Records and Colonial Records.

Pitfalls explicitly addressed: (1) civil vital records don't exist until 1908; (2) territorial censuses (1801/1805/1808) are physical-only at ADAH, creating a 1809–1819 gap; (3) census substitutes index is index-only with no images; (4) the Mobile Bay area was under Spanish/British/French control, requiring different archives; (5) courthouse fires caused record losses; (6) interior Alabama was sparsely settled before Creek War 1813–14.
